title: Everyman
summary: The repository artifact copies a 1993 University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive encoding identified as U-1350-A and later as ota:1679. Its header explicitly names A. C. Cawley's 1961 Manchester edition as the source and says that prefatory and critical matter was omitted and that Ian Lancashire supplied acts and scenes. Current evidence indicates that Cawley's text is an eclectic edition based primarily on the complete Huntington/Britwell Skot printing, with readings from the four early print states known to him; it is not a transcription of the Huth copy alone. The English work survives in four early printed states and no known manuscript witness, but those states occupy five physical objects because Q1 is divided between the separately preserved Douce and Bandinel fragments. The Bandinel leaves were reported only in 2008 and are collateral evidence, not a source Cawley could have consulted in 1961. A complete 1912 facsimile volume containing Huth 32 and the surviving British Library Q2 fragment, the complete OTA 3034 replacement encoding, complete METS and EMOTHE editions, and a complete but rights-restricted Cawley anthology scan are cached privately for verification. Each historical print object, scholarly edition, encoding, repository file, facsimile, access route, and rights statement remains a distinct layer.
review_notes: The repository XML's exact 1485 creation date is a legacy assertion, not a securely established composition date.

The eighteen DIV1 containers and supplied Scene headings were added by Ian Lancashire; their numbering omits Scene 4 and the first DIV1 contains an inherited ‘French scene’ label. They are encoding navigation, not an intrinsic scene hierarchy.
Current USTC numbering and dating differ from the older STC sequence printed in the 2007 METS introduction. Both are preserved without forcing false agreement.

The cached Greg/Farmer volume reproduces all of British Library Huth 32 and the surviving latter two-thirds of British Library C.21.c.17. It does not reproduce the Huntington/Britwell Skot base copy or the Bodleian Pynson fragment.

The Digital Library of India metadata applies ‘1922’ and public-domain fields to a modern Cawley anthology. The scan does contain the inherited series-history line ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922,’ but its title page and second-edition prefaces establish a later Cawley carrier. The provider has misapplied the inherited line as this carrier's date, and its public-domain field is not accepted as rights clearance.

Q1 is not a single unique physical survivor: the Douce fragment preserves four leaves, signature C, lines 684–922, while the separately recovered Bandinel fragment preserves two leaves, signatures B3–B4, lines 429–552. Together they preserve 325 lines from one print state.

The Bandinel leaves were reported in 2008, after Cawley's 1961 edition. As of 2009 they were held by Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman; their current location and access status remain unverified, so no Cawley-to-Bandinel collation edge is asserted.

The lineage schema has no dedicated historical-printed-witness entity type. Its nearest class, scholarly_edition, is therefore used for each early print object or fragment; that technical classification does not claim modern critical editing.
Cawley's 1961 editorial text and apparatus are treated as copyright protected. Public-domain and open-license statements for other digital layers do not transfer to that edition.
The complete METS provider PDF is retained byte-for-byte. It renders and extracts all 106 pages, although qpdf reports recoverable object-offset warnings.

entities.label: General Corpus pinned repository copy of Everyman.xml
entities.description: The 60,504-byte XML artifact used by this repository, pinned through the CME submodule. It contains the complete 921-line text and legacy U-M/OTA header metadata.

entities.label: University of Michigan / Oxford Text Archive Everyman encoding U-1350-A
entities.description: Legacy electronic text distributed by U-M in 1993 and through OTA. The source statement names Cawley 1961; the encoding omits the print preface, contents, introduction, bibliography, alternate readings, and notes, and adds acts/scenes attributed to Ian Lancashire. OTA item 1679 identifies itself as replaced by ota:3034.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman, electronic text U-1350-A / ota:1679 (University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative and Oxford Text Archive, 1993), encoded from A. C. Cawley, ed., Everyman (Manchester, 1961).
bibliographic.title: Everyman

notes: The U-M identifier and OTA legacy number are treated as identifiers for one legacy encoding family rather than as evidence for two independent textual transcriptions.
The current U-M and OTA rights statements differ from the restriction preserved in the legacy XML; all are recorded at their own layer.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive revised Everyman encoding ota:3034
entities.description: The current OTA replacement for ota:1679. Its TEI header says it is a revised version, names Cawley's 1961 edition, and applies CC BY-SA 3.0. Its body has 991 l elements because rubrics, stage matter, split lines, and other units are represented differently; that count is not a revised work-level verse extent.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman, ota:3034, revised TEI version of ota:1679 (Oxford Text Archive).
bibliographic.title: Everyman

entities.label: A. C. Cawley's 1961 edition of Everyman
entities.description: The immediate print source named in the repository XML and OTA replacement. Cawley's edited text occupies 47 pages after xxxviii pages of front matter and includes a facsimile. Evidence reviewed here supports an eclectic text based primarily on the Huntington/Britwell Skot copy rather than Huth alone; the exact 1961 volume itself was not publicly digitized and was not directly inspected.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman, ed. A. C. Cawley, Old and Middle English Texts (Manchester: Manchester University Press, 1961), xxxviii + 47 pp.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

notes: A corrected 1977 reprint with additional bibliography has ISBN 0719001153 and xl + 47 pages; it must not be silently substituted for the exact 1961 carrier named by the XML.
The XML says series number 1, while a Folger record for a 1965 issue says number 3. This manifest does not force one reissue's series numbering onto the 1961 source.

entities.label: Cawley, Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays, second-edition carrier (c. 1959–1960)
entities.description: A complete scanned Everyman's Library anthology edited by A. C. Cawley. Its undated title page and second-edition preface do not establish an exact issue date; catalogs place the Cawley edition around 1959–1960. The direct Everyman source note states that its text is based on the Huntington Skot copy with readings from the other three print objects then known. It is a collateral Cawley edition, not the immediate 1961 Manchester source, and it predates recovery of the Bandinel Q1 leaves.

date_statements.label: second-edition issue
date_statements.notes: The scan's ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922’ statement belongs to series/publication history and cannot date Cawley's modern editorial carrier.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays, ed. A. C. Cawley, Everyman's Library 381, 2nd ed. (London: J. M. Dent; New York: E. P. Dutton, c. 1959–1960), Everyman at printed pp. 205–234.
bibliographic.title: Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays
bibliographic.publisher: J. M. Dent / E. P. Dutton

notes: The scan literally says ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922,’ an inherited series/publication-history line that the provider misapplies as the date of this undated modern Cawley carrier. The provider's public-domain field is not used for rights analysis.
The anthology modernizes presentation and is useful evidence for Cawley's base-copy statement, but it is not assumed to be textually identical with the 1961 original-spelling Manchester edition.

entities.label: Richard Pynson Q1 Douce fragment, Bodleian Library (USTC 501308)
entities.description: One of two detached physical survivors of the earliest Pynson print state, Q1. The Bodleian Douce fragment contains four leaves of signature C and preserves lines 684–922, with the top six lines of each leaf lost through trimming. The separately modeled Bandinel leaves preserve another portion of the same state. Current USTC assigns STC 10604 and ESTC S114635 but lists only the Bodleian holding; the 2007 METS edition predates recovery of the Bandinel leaves.

bibliographic.citation: [Everyman] ([London]: Richard Pynson, c. 1515), surviving Bodleian fragment; USTC 501308, STC 10604, ESTC S114635.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

holding.notes: The reviewed public USTC record names the Bodleian but leaves its shelfmark blank; ‘Douce Fragment’ is the designation used by METS and EMOTHE. No shelfmark is invented.

physical_description.extent: Four leaves, signature C; lines 684–922 survive, with the top six lines of each leaf trimmed away

notes: The Douce and Bandinel fragments together preserve 325 lines of Q1; they are two physical objects from one print state, not two editions.

entities.label: Richard Pynson Q1 Bandinel fragment (signatures B3–B4)
entities.description: A separately preserved two-leaf survivor of the same earliest Pynson state as the Bodleian Douce fragment. The Bandinel leaves are signatures B3–B4 and preserve lines 429–552. Arthur Freeman reported them in 2008 among the recovered Bandinel fragments; they were held by Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman as of 2009, but their current location is unverified. Because their recovery postdates Cawley's 1961 edition, they are collateral Q1 evidence and not a source for Cawley's text.

date_statements.label: current USTC dating of print state
date_statements.notes: USTC 501308 currently lists only the Bodleian survivor; this date is applied at state level, not as a cataloged holding record for the Bandinel object.

bibliographic.citation: [Everyman] ([London]: Richard Pynson, c. 1515 or c. 1518–1519), Bandinel fragment, signatures B3–B4, lines 429–552; Q1 / STC 10604 / USTC state 501308.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

notes: Together with the four Douce leaves, the Bandinel leaves bring Q1's surviving extent to 325 lines.
No current institutional holding, shelfmark, catalog record, or public facsimile was verified.

entities.label: Richard Pynson Everyman fragment, British Library C.21.c.17 (USTC 501897)
entities.description: An incomplete Pynson print preserving roughly the latter two-thirds of the play. Current USTC dates it c. 1526 and assigns STC 10604.5 and ESTC S4670. The entire surviving fragment is reproduced in the cached Greg/Farmer facsimile.

bibliographic.citation: [Everyman] ([London]: Richard Pynson, c. 1526), British Library C.21.c.17; USTC 501897, STC 10604.5, ESTC S4670.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

physical_description.extent: Incomplete; roughly the latter two-thirds of the play

entities.label: John Skot complete Everyman, Huntington Library 14195 (USTC 502061)
entities.description: A complete Skot printing, formerly at Britwell Court. Current USTC dates it c. 1528 and assigns STC 10606 and ESTC S105555. Cawley's collateral anthology expressly identifies the Huntington Skot copy as its base; the 1961 source and XML readings support it as the principal base for the immediate Cawley lineage as well.

bibliographic.citation: The Somonynge of Everyman ([London]: John Skot, c. 1528), Huntington Library 14195; USTC 502061, STC 10606, ESTC S105555.
bibliographic.title: The Somonynge of Everyman

notes: The 2007 METS early-edition list assigns this same Huntington/Britwell state a broader c. 1530–1535 date; current USTC gives 1528.

entities.label: John Skot complete Everyman, British Library Huth 32 (USTC 502713)
entities.description: A complete Skot printing held as Huth 32. Current USTC dates it c. 1535 and assigns STC 10606.5 and ESTC S114637. It is the base text of the 2007 METS edition and is reproduced completely in the cached 1912 facsimile, but it is not Cawley's sole or principal base.

bibliographic.citation: The Somonyng of Everyman ([London]: John Skot, c. 1535), British Library Huth 32; USTC 502713, STC 10606.5, ESTC S114637.
bibliographic.title: The Somonyng of Everyman

notes: The 2007 METS volume used older STC 10605 and c. 1530–1535 for Huth; the current USTC maps the copy to STC 10606.5 and 1535.

entities.label: Greg and Farmer's 1912 facsimile of the Huth Skot print and British Library Pynson fragment
entities.description: A complete scan of the 1912 facsimile volume. It reproduces the complete Huth 32 Skot printing and the complete surviving British Library C.21.c.17 Pynson fragment, including blank/interleaf surfaces. The scan is a historical monochrome facsimile publication, not a modern full-color direct digital surrogate of either original.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman: A Facsimile Reproduction of the Editions of John Skot and Richard Pynson, ed. W. W. Greg, with a note by John S. Farmer (London, 1912).
bibliographic.title: Everyman: A Facsimile Reproduction of the Editions of John Skot and Richard Pynson
bibliographic.publisher: T. C. & E. C. Jack / Tudor Facsimile Texts

entities.reproduction_format: Historical photographic facsimile digitized as a 138-page PDF and a 138-image JP2 ZIP

entities.label: METS Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc (2007)
entities.description: A modern parallel scholarly edition edited by Clifford Davidson, Martin W. Walsh, and Ton J. Broos. Its English copytext is British Library Huth 32, corrected from the Huntington Skot copy and the two then-known Pynson state representatives, British Library C.21.c.17 and the Douce fragment. Published in 2007, it predates Freeman's report of the separate Bandinel Q1 leaves. It supplies strong scholarly comparison for source direction, early-print census as then known, editorial readings, and the distinction between Huth and Cawley's eclectic text.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc, ed. Clifford Davidson, Martin W. Walsh, and Ton J. Broos (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2007).
bibliographic.title: Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc
bibliographic.pages: 106-page downloadable edition; English Everyman on printed pp. 17–77

entities.reproduction_format: Born-digital reading edition and complete downloadable PDF derived from the print edition

entities.label: EMOTHE critical modernized edition of The Summoning of Everyman (2019)
entities.description: Jesús Tronch Pérez's public critical modernized edition based on Q3, the complete Huntington Skot copy, with a TEI apparatus recording Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4 readings and modern editorial sources. Its witness analysis incorporates the post-Cawley Bandinel discovery and distinguishes the two physical fragments of Q1.

bibliographic.citation: The Summoning of Everyman: A Critical, Modernized Edition Based on the Third Quarto, ed. Jesús Tronch Pérez (Valencia: ARTELOPE–EMOTHE, Universitat de València, 2019; text-only edition first published 2018), EMOTHE 0460.
bibliographic.title: The Summoning of Everyman: A Critical, Modernized Edition Based on the Third Quarto

entities.reproduction_format: Born-digital critical modernized reading edition with a complete downloadable TEI apparatus
notes: The TEI embeds DOI 10.5281/zenodo.3958901, but that DOI returned an unregistered-identifier response during review; the live EMOTHE landing page and exact TEI URL remain authoritative access routes.

access.access_method: Consult Freeman's peer-reviewed article and EMOTHE's public witness analysis. These describe the fragment but do not provide a verified current object catalog or complete public facsimile.

notes: The Bandinel leaves were held by Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman as of 2009. Current ownership, holding, shelfmark, image access, and reproduction terms were not verified.

rights.component: Repository-authored XML/code layer insofar as copyright is controlled by the CME repository contributors
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide dedication; legal effect may vary by jurisdiction
rights.basis: The exact pinned CME LICENSE dedicates the software/source to the public domain under the Unlicense.
notes: This dedication cannot license copyright that the repository contributors do not own. In particular, it does not clear Cawley's modern editorial contribution or METS content.

rights.component: Current U-M digital item and encoded text as presented by the provider
rights.basis: The current U-M item page expressly marks the materials public domain and links the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: U-M also describes access as for educational and research purposes. The assertion conflicts with the modern Cawley source layer and the legacy personal-scholarly-use statement; it is not transferred to Cawley's edition.

rights.component: Legacy U-1350-A distribution represented by the pinned XML header
rights.basis: The legacy XML availability statement says the text was made available through OTA ‘for personal scholarly use only.’
notes: This is a historical use condition, not a Creative Commons license. It is recorded alongside, not silently replaced by, later provider statements.

rights.component: OTA repository item ota:1679 encoding and item metadata
rights.basis: The official OTA 1679 item record displays Creative Commons Attribution 3.0.
notes: License scope is the OTA digital item, not Cawley's 1961 editorial work.

rights.basis: Both the OTA item record and the cached TEI header identify Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0.
notes: The open license applies to the revised encoding layer and does not independently clear protected editorial readings inherited from Cawley.

rights.component: A. C. Cawley's 1961 editorial text, introduction, apparatus, notes, and selection
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom, United States, and other treaty jurisdictions
rights.basis: The carrier is a modern 1961 scholarly edition, and no public-domain basis, open license, or rights waiver for Cawley's original editorial contribution was found. It is conservatively treated as protected.
notes: The underlying medieval work and early print content are public domain, but Cawley's original editorial contribution is a separate protected layer.

rights.component: Cawley's modern editorial text, notes, arrangement, and prefatory matter in the anthology
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom, United States, and other treaty jurisdictions
rights.basis: Direct inspection identifies a modern A. C. Cawley second edition. Its literal ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922’ line is inherited publication history, not the date of this undated modern carrier, and Cawley's editorial contribution remains within ordinary copyright terms.
notes: The provider has misapplied the scan's inherited 1922 line as the carrier date and labels the item public domain. Those metadata fields do not clear the modern Cawley layer. Keep the scan private and ignored; do not redistribute it from the repository.

rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the c. 1515 fragment
rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.
notes: This does not override contractual terms or separate rights in modern photographs and subscription databases.

rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the early-sixteenth-century Bandinel fragment
rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.
notes: This conclusion concerns the historical printed content only. Current ownership, access conditions, and any rights or terms applying to modern photographs remain unknown.

rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the c. 1526 fragment
rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.

rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the c. 1528 complete print
rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.

rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the c. 1535 complete print
rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.

rights.component: 1912 facsimile publication, editorial arrangement, and reproductions as digitized
rights.basis: The facsimile volume was published in 1912 and is public domain in the United States by publication date.
notes: Internet Archive provides free access but the reviewed item metadata does not supply a specific modern open license. Public-domain status is based on the 1912 publication date, not an inferred provider license.

rights.component: 2007 METS edition, including edited English and Dutch texts, translation, introduction, notes, and downloadable PDF
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other treaty jurisdictions
rights.basis: METS expressly states that all editions are under copyright and that current digital editions are for individual use only; reprinting requires permission.
notes: METS separately licenses its XML as CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. That XML-specific license is not transferred to the cached PDF or to all edition content.

rights.component: EMOTHE's distributed TEI encoding of the critical modernized edition
rights.basis: The complete TEI header expressly licenses the encoding under Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–NoDerivatives 4.0 International.
notes: The license does not change the public-domain status of the underlying early printed text, does not independently clear third-party editions cited in the apparatus, and its no-derivatives condition matters for redistribution of modified TEI.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cawley 1961 preface, table of contents, introduction, bibliography, alternate readings, and notes
editorial_practices.representation: Omitted; only the edited play text and selected source/header information are encoded
editorial_practices.description: The XML editorial declaration explicitly enumerates the print components excluded from the digital item.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire encoded item relative to the 1961 source edition

editorial_practices.source_feature: Continuous print play without the encoded acts/scenes
editorial_practices.representation: Eighteen DIV1 scene containers and supplied visible Scene headings attributed to Ian Lancashire
editorial_practices.description: The navigation hierarchy is a modern encoding intervention. Its numbering skips Scene 4 and runs through Scene 19; the first DIV1 additionally preserves an anomalous ‘French scene’ heading.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Four early print states as represented by the two complete Skot copies and two Pynson fragment objects known to Cawley; the separate Bandinel Q1 leaves were not recovered until decades later
editorial_practices.representation: Eclectic edited text based primarily on the Huntington/Britwell Skot copy, with readings and emendations from comparison copies
editorial_practices.description: The exact 1961 preface remains uninspected, so the base-copy claim is supported from the OTA header, Cawley's directly inspected related anthology, and diagnostic textual variants rather than asserted as an exact quotation from the 1961 volume. No relation to the post-1961 Bandinel discovery is implied.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Verse lines, prose title rubric, speakers, stage directions, blank or split lines, and colophon
editorial_practices.representation: 991 TEI l elements across nine signature-based divs
editorial_practices.description: The replacement encoding models more source units as l than the legacy XML's 921 numbered verse lines. The metric is encoding-specific and must not be promoted to the work's verse extent.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Huth 32 copytext with readings from Huntington, British Library Q2, and the Douce Q1 fragment; the 2007 edition predates Freeman's Bandinel report
editorial_practices.representation: Huth-based reading text corrected where warranted, with principal variants in textual notes
editorial_practices.description: The editors explicitly identify Huth 32 as copytext and the other three print-state representatives then known as comparison witnesses. The edition cannot evidence consultation of the Bandinel leaves discovered afterward.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Huth abbreviations and early-print letter/speech-heading variation
editorial_practices.representation: Abbreviations expanded; speech headings and u/v and i/j regularized
editorial_practices.description: The introduction states these reader-oriented normalizations directly, distinguishing the edition from a diplomatic facsimile.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Q3 Huntington base text with readings from Q1's Douce and Bandinel fragments, Q2 British Library, Q4 Huth, and modern editions
editorial_practices.representation: Modernized reading text with an internal TEI critical apparatus keyed to Q1–Q4 and modern-edition sigla
editorial_practices.description: The complete TEI identifies Q3 as its base, separately describes the two physical Q1 fragments, and encodes substantive and other variant readings. Its witness dates preserve a scholarly chronology that differs from current USTC.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete 921-line modernized Everyman text and critical apparatus

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source file Everyman.xml, CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the repository artifact, U-M/OTA identifiers and 1993 date, legacy use restriction, Cawley 1961 source, omitted apparatus, supplied Lancashire scene divisions, legacy 1485 date, complete 921-line text, and Skot colophon.

evidence.citation: Everyman.xml SOURCEDESC and ENCODINGDESC
evidence.summary: Names A. C. Cawley's Everyman (Manchester University Press, 1961), xxxviii + 47 pages, as source; states that prefatory, bibliographic, alternate-reading, and note matter is omitted and that Ian Lancashire added acts/scenes absent from print.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual audit of pinned Everyman.xml
evidence.locator: Complete XML tag, numbering, opener, and closer audit
evidence.summary: Verified 18 DIV1, 19 HEAD, 921 L, 28 PB, 211 SP/SPEAKER, four STAGE, nine signature MILESTONE, and three FOREIGN elements; confirmed the missing supplied Scene 4 and first-DIV ‘French scene’ anomaly.

evidence.citation: Pinned CME Git object state for Everyman.xml
evidence.summary: Fixes the repository path, source-submodule commit, blob object, byte size, and SHA-256 used by the manifest.

evidence.citation: CME LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.summary: Exact Unlicense text dedicating repository-controlled software/source contributions to the public domain, with warranty disclaimer.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, ‘Everyman,’ Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: Official current access page for Everyman / U-1350-A; identifies Anonymous, Manchester University Press 1961, public access, educational/research purpose, public-domain assertion, and No Copyright–United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item ota:1679, Everyman
evidence.summary: Official legacy item record identifying public availability, CC BY 3.0, SGML/header files, and replacement by ota:3034. Direct bitstream attempts redirected to the landing page during review.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item ota:3034, Everyman
evidence.summary: Official replacement record providing XML, HTML, text, EPUB, and MOBI derivatives and displaying CC BY-SA 3.0.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive ota:3034 original TEI XML
evidence.summary: Complete cached replacement encoding. Its header says it revises ota:1679, names Cawley 1961 and Ian Lancashire, describes the Manchester edition as drawn from a Skot print, records the 2010 header normalization, and states CC BY-SA 3.0.

evidence.citation: Local structural audit of cached ota:3034 TEI XML
evidence.locator: TEI body tag and line-unit counts
evidence.summary: Verified nine signature-based divs and 991 l elements; inspected how prose rubric, speech/stage matter, split lines, and colophon are represented, establishing that 991 is an encoding-unit count rather than the work's verse-line extent.

evidence.citation: Open British National Bibliography, Everyman, OBNB GB6111852
evidence.summary: National bibliographic data confirming Manchester University Press, 1961, English language, and 47-page extent for the source edition.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 31514, Everyman, ed. A. C. Cawley
evidence.summary: Catalog record for a 1965 Manchester issue, xxxviii + 47 pages, call number PR2411 .E8 1965; used to distinguish later issues from the exact 1961 carrier.

evidence.citation: CiNii Books BA20924946, Everyman, ed. A. C. Cawley
evidence.summary: Catalog evidence for the corrected 1977 reprint, ISBN 0719001153, xl + 47 pages, with additional bibliography; prevents conflation with the 1961 source.

evidence.citation: AbeBooks used-copy aggregation for ISBN 9780719001154 / 0719001153
evidence.summary: Current used-copy purchase route. Listings mix publication years and issues, so each seller copy requires verification before use as the exact 1961 source.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of the complete DLI scan of A. C. Cawley, ed., Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays, second edition
evidence.summary: Verified Cawley's title and second-edition prefaces, the undated modern carrier, the literal inherited line ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922,’ the Everyman work boundaries, and the direct source note: four early print states then represented by two Pynson fragment objects and two complete Skot copies, with the text based on the Huntington Skot copy and a few readings from the other then-known copies.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive / Digital Library of India metadata for in.ernet.dli.2015.458516
evidence.summary: Preserves provider file inventory and the provider's 1922/public-domain fields. The 1922 text is genuinely present in the scan as inherited Everyman's Library publication history, but the provider misapplies it as the date of Cawley's undated modern second-edition carrier; neither field supplies rights clearance for that layer.

evidence.citation: WorldCat OCLC 368406 and Google Books catalog records for Cawley's Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays issues
evidence.summary: Catalog evidence for closely matching Cawley second-edition carriers: a 1959 Dutton issue and 1959/1960 Dent issues, all 266 pages. Combined with the scan's Darnell Professor title page and second-edition preface, these records support the cautious c. 1959–1960 date without identifying the exact undated issue conclusively.

evidence.citation: Universal Short Title Catalogue records for the four early English Everyman printings
evidence.summary: Current bibliographic evidence for Pynson c. 1515 / USTC 501308 / Bodleian, Pynson c. 1526 / 501897 / British Library, Skot c. 1528 / 502061 / Huntington 14195, and Skot c. 1535 / 502713 / British Library Huth 32, including ESTC/STC identifiers, format, extent, and EEBO links. USTC 501308 currently names only the Bodleian survivor and does not separately locate the Bandinel leaves of the same Q1 state.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, ‘Everyman’
evidence.summary: Scholarly repertory census reporting 921 verse lines, morality genre, four early print states, and no English manuscript witness. It is used as state-level bibliography, not to infer that each state survives in only one physical object.

evidence.citation: Arthur Freeman, ‘Everyman and Others, Part II: The Bandinel Fragments,’ The Library 9, no. 4 (December 2008): 397–427
evidence.locator: Abstract and article bibliographic record; Everyman fragment discussion
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed report of two hitherto unknown leaves from the first Pynson edition of Everyman among the recovered Bandinel fragments. Freeman dates the state c. 1518–1519; later EMOTHE witness analysis maps the leaves to signatures B3–B4 and lines 429–552.
notes: The article is the discovery report; a complete freely accessible article copy was not verified, so claims beyond its public abstract are cross-checked against the open EMOTHE edition.

evidence.citation: Jesús Tronch Pérez, ed., The Summoning of Everyman: A Critical, Modernized Edition Based on the Third Quarto (Valencia: ARTELOPE–EMOTHE, Universitat de València, 2019; text-only edition first published 2018), EMOTHE 0460
evidence.locator: Complete TEI header, listWit, edition introduction, 921-line text, and critical apparatus
evidence.summary: Complete open critical modernized edition based on Q3 with Q1–Q4 and modern-edition apparatus. Its witness analysis distinguishes four Douce leaves (signature C, lines 684–922) from two Bandinel leaves (B3–B4, lines 429–552), reports 325 surviving Q1 lines across the two objects, records Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman as the Bandinel holders in 2009, and dates Q1 c. 1518–1519. The TEI header licenses the encoding CC BY-NC-ND 4.0.
notes: The TEI's embedded DOI 10.5281/zenodo.3958901 returned an unregistered-identifier response during review; the live EMOTHE page and exact XML URL remained accessible.

evidence.citation: Direct visual and structural inspection of W. W. Greg and John S. Farmer, Everyman facsimile (1912), Internet Archive PDF
evidence.locator: Complete 138-page PDF; Huth pp. 13–74; Pynson pp. 79–116
evidence.summary: Verified the complete 1912 volume, Farmer's source note, section boundaries, blank/interleaf structure, complete Huth text and device, entire surviving British Library Pynson fragment and colophon, and exact work-bearing page sequences.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for cu31924013324276
evidence.summary: Provider metadata and file inventory for the 1912 Greg/Farmer facsimile, including direct PDF and JP2 ZIP objects and checksums.

evidence.citation: Clifford Davidson, Martin W. Walsh, and Ton J. Broos, eds., Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc (2007)
evidence.locator: Introduction pp. 1–11; Early Editions; English text; textual notes
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence for English translation-adaptation from Elckerlijc, unknown exact exemplar, four early print states, Huth copytext, Huntington and then-known Pynson comparison objects, normalization practices, and significant variants. Published in 2007, it predates Freeman's report of the separate Bandinel Q1 leaves.

evidence.citation: METS 2007 textual notes to Everyman compared with pinned Everyman.xml
evidence.locator: Textual Notes to Everyman, especially variants at lines 14, 111, 301, 317, 656, and 666
evidence.summary: Shows the XML/Cawley line taking Huntington readings and explicit Cawley emendations rather than following Huth alone: for example ‘the soule,’ ‘Haue ado that thou,’ ‘endynge,’ omission of witness ‘go,’ and Knowledge speaker headings.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of the complete METS downloadable PDF
evidence.locator: All 106 PDF pages; English text PDF pp. 19, 21, 23 … 79
evidence.summary: Verified complete edition coverage and exact work portion, facing-page English/Dutch layout, title image, introduction, explanatory notes, textual notes, and bibliography. All pages render and extract; qpdf's recoverable object-offset warnings were documented without modifying provider bytes.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, ‘Copyright & Citation’
evidence.summary: States that METS editions are copyrighted, digital editions are for individual use only, reprinting requires permission, and METS XML is separately licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

open_questions.question: Can the exact 1961 Manchester volume be inspected to verify its preface, base-copy declaration, apparatus, corrections, and differences from Cawley's anthology and later reprints?
next_steps: Obtain or borrow the exact 1961 edition rather than a 1965 issue or corrected 1977 reprint.
Photograph or transcribe its title verso, preface, textual principles, and apparatus within applicable law and provider terms.
Compare diagnostic XML readings with the 1961 text and all four early states, while keeping the post-1961 Bandinel leaves collateral rather than imputing them to Cawley.
notes: The present Huntington-base edge is deliberately supported/medium rather than confirmed/high until the immediate volume is checked.

open_questions.question: Is a free, complete, directly downloadable whole-object facsimile available for Huntington Library 14195, Cawley's principal Skot base copy?
next_steps: Check Huntington's current digital catalog by shelfmark 14195 and USTC 502061.
Use the linked EEBO record through an entitled institution if available.
Ask Huntington imaging services whether a complete color reproduction can be ordered and what publication terms apply.
notes: A current Huntington Digital Library search by title and shelfmark did not expose a matching public object. Huntington states that only a fraction of its holdings are digitized and offers imaging or virtual reading-room routes, so absence from public search is not evidence that no images exist.

open_questions.question: What is the current exact Bodleian shelfmark for the Pynson ‘Douce Fragment,’ and can the complete surviving object be obtained as a public or requestable facsimile?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections to resolve the historical ‘Douce Fragment’ designation against current catalog data.
Request a complete-object image quotation and an exact work/surface inventory.
Retain the USTC record and METS designation without inventing a shelfmark.

open_questions.question: Where are the Bandinel Q1 leaves now held, and can the complete two-leaf object be accessed or reproduced?
next_steps: Trace ownership after Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman's reported 2009 holding through provenance scholarship and current institutional catalogs.
Ask Freeman's publisher, relevant rare-book repositories, or the editors who facilitated EMOTHE materials for a current holding and shelfmark.
Do not infer current ownership or image rights from the 2008 discovery article or 2009 EMOTHE provenance note.

open_questions.question: How should the current U-M NoC-US assertion, OTA Creative Commons licenses, legacy personal-scholarly-use restriction, and protected Cawley source be reconciled for downstream redistribution?
next_steps: Keep each statement scoped to its own carrier and contribution layer.
Do not treat U-M, OTA, or repository licensing as clearance for Cawley's modern editorial choices.
Seek qualified legal advice or rights-holder permission before redistributing a close reproduction of protected editorial matter.

open_questions.question: Can the c. 1515 USTC date and c. 1518–1519 Freeman/EMOTHE date for Q1 be reconciled, and will revised cataloging represent both physical Q1 fragments while retaining one print-state identity?
next_steps: Recheck USTC, ESTC/CERL, holding-library records, and new bibliographical studies during future reviews.
Preserve Freeman/EMOTHE and old METS/STC chronologies as competing scholarly evidence while using current USTC identifiers for discovery.
Keep four print states distinct from five surviving physical objects even if catalogs continue to list only the Douce holding for USTC 501308.

open_questions.question: Which exact Dent/Dutton issue is represented by the undated DLI Cawley anthology scan?
next_steps: Compare collation, binding, title verso, and second-edition preface with authenticated 1959 and 1960 cataloged copies.
Continue to label the cached carrier c. 1959–1960 and do not apply its inherited ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922’ line as the date of Cawley's edition.
